.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:225:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:1:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:226:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:3:### Efficacy
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:227:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:5:In the SURPASS-1 trial of patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone, tirzepatide at 5, 10, and 15 mg doses reduced HbA1c by −1.87%, −1.89%, and −2.07% from baseline versus a 0.04% increase with placebo at 40 weeks.[1][2] Tirzepatide also demonstrated superior efficacy versus basal insulins; in SURPASS-3, it reduced HbA1c by −1.93% to −2.37% across doses versus −1.34% with insulin degludec over 52 weeks, and in SURPASS-4, it reduced HbA1c by −2.24% to −2.58% versus −1.44% with insulin glargine.[1][3][4] For patients on basal insulin with inadequate control, adding tirzepatide in SURPASS-6 was more effective than adding prandial insulin lispro, reducing HbA1c by −2.1% versus −1.1% and reducing body weight by −6.7 to −11.0 kg versus a 3.2 kg gain.[1][5] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets, with 57% of patients on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7.0%, weight loss >5%, systolic blood pressure <130 mmHg) versus 34% on semaglutide.[6] Efficacy was consistent across key subgroups; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, greater HbA1c reductions were seen in patients with higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%), and greater weight loss was seen in those with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[7] A pre-planned analysis showed tirzepatide enabled faster achievement of glycemic and weight loss thresholds than semaglutide 1 mg or insulin degludec, with a median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 and 12.1 weeks, respectively.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:228:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:7:### Safety
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:229:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:9:Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide across phase 3 trials in type 2 diabetes.[1][9] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in an Asia-Pacific population, gastrointestinal adverse event incidence was generally similar across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, diabetes duration, and concomitant oral medications.[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of nine RCTs concluded the gastrointestinal adverse event profile of tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1 receptor agonists, with incidences of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea higher than placebo but similar to active comparators like semaglutide and dulaglutide.[10] A systematic review and meta-analysis concluded tirzepatide did not increase events of blood glucose <54 mg/dL or <70 mg/dL compared to control overall.[11] However, when tirzepatide is added to insulin or sulfonylureas, the risk increases; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, where patients were on metformin with or without a sulfonylurea, the incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose ≤70 mg/dL or severe) was higher in patients using a concomitant sulfonylurea (e.g., 43.1% with tirzepatide 5 mg) compared to those on metformin alone (6.6% with tirzepatide 5 mg).[7] One meta-analysis specifically noted tirzepatide 15 mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83), while another network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide.[10][12] In clinical trials, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) were reported; for instance, the SURMOUNT-4 trial specifically stated no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma or pancreatic cancer were reported.[13] However, when compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists, tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer.[14] A 2026 evidence review summarizing multiple large database studies concluded there is no evidence of increased thyroid cancer risk with GLP-1 receptor agonist use, including tirzepatide, over 1.8-3.0 years of follow-up.[15] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal cardiovascular and obesity outcome trials, including the SURPASS program, did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[16]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:230:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:11:### Comparative
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:231:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:13:A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide found that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, with tirzepatide 15 mg ranked as the most efficacious treatment.[17] Tirzepatide also enabled more patients to achieve stringent glycemic targets faster than semaglutide 1 mg, with a median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 weeks and to achieve HbA1c ≤6.5% of 12.1 weeks versus 15.7 weeks.[8] A meta-analysis of direct comparative studies in overweight and obese adults, which included populations with and without type 2 diabetes, found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide for weight reduction, with a standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI 0.52 to 0.92) and greater odds of achieving ≥10% weight loss (OR 0.21, 95% CI 0.06 to 0.78).[18] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 reported that tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving an intensive weight loss target of >15% compared to semaglutide (OR 3.86, 95% CI 2.69 to 5.55).[6] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and significant weight loss, while insulin degludec was associated with weight gain.[3] Furthermore, a network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the GLP-1 RAs dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <54 mg/dL) was low and similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 1 mg.[20] The network meta-analysis comparing subcutaneous agents found both tirzepatide and semaglutide increased the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to placebo, with no increased risk of serious adverse events or severe hypoglycemia for either.[17]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:232:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:15:### Dose Response
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:233:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:17:Tirzepatide demonstrates a clear dose-dependent effect on both glycemic control and weight reduction in patients with type 2 diabetes.[1][21][22] Another meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with tirzepatide 15 mg achieving the best efficacy.[21] The time to achieve glycemic and weight loss targets is also dose-responsive and faster with higher tirzepatide doses compared to active comparators.[8] A pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to first achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all tirzepatide doses, compared to 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec.[8] Furthermore, the median time to achieve ≥5% weight loss in SURPASS-2 was 16.0 weeks with tirzepatide 5 mg and 12.4 weeks with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses, compared to 24.0 weeks with semaglutide 1 mg.[8] Subgroup analyses indicate that the efficacy of tirzepatide is generally consistent across different patient demographics, though some variations exist.[7] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, patients with a higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%) experienced significantly greater HbA1c reductions with all tirzepatide doses, while patients with a higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater weight reductions across all doses.[7] An indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater HbA1c and body weight reductions compared to semaglutide 2.4 mg (an obesity dose in a related population), while tirzepatide 5 mg showed similar efficacy to semaglutide 2.4 mg.[23]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:234:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:19:### Regulatory
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:235:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the trade name Mounjaro in 2022 as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24][25] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The FDA label carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, based on findings in rats, and contraindicates tirzepatide in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2.[26] The FDA later approved tirzepatide for chronic weight management under the trade name Zepbound in 2023, indicated as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for adults with obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥27 kg/m²) with at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27][28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for Mounjaro valid throughout the EU on 15 September 2022.[29] The EMA's approved indication for type 2 diabetes is for the treatment of adults, adolescents, and children aged 10 years and above with insufficiently controlled disease as an adjunct to diet and exercise, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic products.[30] The EMA also approved an indication for weight management, defined as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31][30][32] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific problems associated with obesity, or a BMI below 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE's recommendation is contingent upon a commercial arrangement that makes tirzepatide available to the NHS at a cost-effective price.[33][34] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide as an option for people with a BMI of more than 35 and at least one weight-related illness, with an initial rollout prioritized for those in specialist services.[35] Key labeling warnings and precautions are consistent across authorities and include risks of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, hypersensitivity reactions, and hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin or insulin secretagogues.[36][26][30] The FDA and EMA labels also warn that tirzepatide delays gastric emptying, which can impact the absorption of concomitantly administered oral medications, necessitating guidance for patients using oral contraceptives.[25][37][30] The Canadian Product Monograph for Zepbound similarly contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma and warns of gastrointestinal adverse reactions and the potential for drug interactions due to delayed gastric emptying.[38]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:376:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command '$i=0; Get-Content -LiteralPath outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md | ForEach-Object { $i++; '"'{0,4}: {1}' -f "'$i, $_ }' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:382:   1: # Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:386:   5: In the SURPASS-1 trial of patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone, tirzepatide at 5, 10, and 15 mg doses reduced HbA1c by −1.87%, −1.89%, and −2.07% from baseline versus a 0.04% increase with placebo at 40 weeks.[1][2] Tirzepatide also demonstrated superior efficacy versus basal insulins; in SURPASS-3, it reduced HbA1c by −1.93% to −2.37% across doses versus −1.34% with insulin degludec over 52 weeks, and in SURPASS-4, it reduced HbA1c by −2.24% to −2.58% versus −1.44% with insulin glargine.[1][3][4] For patients on basal insulin with inadequate control, adding tirzepatide in SURPASS-6 was more effective than adding prandial insulin lispro, reducing HbA1c by −2.1% versus −1.1% and reducing body weight by −6.7 to −11.0 kg versus a 3.2 kg gain.[1][5] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets, with 57% of patients on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7.0%, weight loss >5%, systolic blood pressure <130 mmHg) versus 34% on semaglutide.[6] Efficacy was consistent across key subgroups; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, greater HbA1c reductions were seen in patients with higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%), and greater weight loss was seen in those with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[7] A pre-planned analysis showed tirzepatide enabled faster achievement of glycemic and weight loss thresholds than semaglutide 1 mg or insulin degludec, with a median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 and 12.1 weeks, respectively.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:390:   9: Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide across phase 3 trials in type 2 diabetes.[1][9] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in an Asia-Pacific population, gastrointestinal adverse event incidence was generally similar across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, diabetes duration, and concomitant oral medications.[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of nine RCTs concluded the gastrointestinal adverse event profile of tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1 receptor agonists, with incidences of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea higher than placebo but similar to active comparators like semaglutide and dulaglutide.[10] A systematic review and meta-analysis concluded tirzepatide did not increase events of blood glucose <54 mg/dL or <70 mg/dL compared to control overall.[11] However, when tirzepatide is added to insulin or sulfonylureas, the risk increases; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, where patients were on metformin with or without a sulfonylurea, the incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose ≤70 mg/dL or severe) was higher in patients using a concomitant sulfonylurea (e.g., 43.1% with tirzepatide 5 mg) compared to those on metformin alone (6.6% with tirzepatide 5 mg).[7] One meta-analysis specifically noted tirzepatide 15 mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83), while another network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide.[10][12] In clinical trials, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) were reported; for instance, the SURMOUNT-4 trial specifically stated no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma or pancreatic cancer were reported.[13] However, when compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists, tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer.[14] A 2026 evidence review summarizing multiple large database studies concluded there is no evidence of increased thyroid cancer risk with GLP-1 receptor agonist use, including tirzepatide, over 1.8-3.0 years of follow-up.[15] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal cardiovascular and obesity outcome trials, including the SURPASS program, did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[16]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:394:  13: A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide found that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, with tirzepatide 15 mg ranked as the most efficacious treatment.[17] Tirzepatide also enabled more patients to achieve stringent glycemic targets faster than semaglutide 1 mg, with a median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 weeks and to achieve HbA1c ≤6.5% of 12.1 weeks versus 15.7 weeks.[8] A meta-analysis of direct comparative studies in overweight and obese adults, which included populations with and without type 2 diabetes, found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide for weight reduction, with a standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI 0.52 to 0.92) and greater odds of achieving ≥10% weight loss (OR 0.21, 95% CI 0.06 to 0.78).[18] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 reported that tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving an intensive weight loss target of >15% compared to semaglutide (OR 3.86, 95% CI 2.69 to 5.55).[6] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and significant weight loss, while insulin degludec was associated with weight gain.[3] Furthermore, a network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the GLP-1 RAs dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <54 mg/dL) was low and similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 1 mg.[20] The network meta-analysis comparing subcutaneous agents found both tirzepatide and semaglutide increased the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to placebo, with no increased risk of serious adverse events or severe hypoglycemia for either.[17]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:398:  17: Tirzepatide demonstrates a clear dose-dependent effect on both glycemic control and weight reduction in patients with type 2 diabetes.[1][21][22] Another meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with tirzepatide 15 mg achieving the best efficacy.[21] The time to achieve glycemic and weight loss targets is also dose-responsive and faster with higher tirzepatide doses compared to active comparators.[8] A pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to first achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all tirzepatide doses, compared to 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec.[8] Furthermore, the median time to achieve ≥5% weight loss in SURPASS-2 was 16.0 weeks with tirzepatide 5 mg and 12.4 weeks with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses, compared to 24.0 weeks with semaglutide 1 mg.[8] Subgroup analyses indicate that the efficacy of tirzepatide is generally consistent across different patient demographics, though some variations exist.[7] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, patients with a higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%) experienced significantly greater HbA1c reductions with all tirzepatide doses, while patients with a higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater weight reductions across all doses.[7] An indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater HbA1c and body weight reductions compared to semaglutide 2.4 mg (an obesity dose in a related population), while tirzepatide 5 mg showed similar efficacy to semaglutide 2.4 mg.[23]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:402:  21: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the trade name Mounjaro in 2022 as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24][25] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The FDA label carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, based on findings in rats, and contraindicates tirzepatide in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2.[26] The FDA later approved tirzepatide for chronic weight management under the trade name Zepbound in 2023, indicated as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for adults with obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥27 kg/m²) with at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27][28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for Mounjaro valid throughout the EU on 15 September 2022.[29] The EMA's approved indication for type 2 diabetes is for the treatment of adults, adolescents, and children aged 10 years and above with insufficiently controlled disease as an adjunct to diet and exercise, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic products.[30] The EMA also approved an indication for weight management, defined as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31][30][32] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific problems associated with obesity, or a BMI below 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE's recommendation is contingent upon a commercial arrangement that makes tirzepatide available to the NHS at a cost-effective price.[33][34] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide as an option for people with a BMI of more than 35 and at least one weight-related illness, with an initial rollout prioritized for those in specialist services.[35] Key labeling warnings and precautions are consistent across authorities and include risks of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, hypersensitivity reactions, and hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin or insulin secretagogues.[36][26][30] The FDA and EMA labels also warn that tirzepatide delays gastric emptying, which can impact the absorption of concomitantly administered oral medications, necessitating guidance for patients using oral contraceptives.[25][37][30] The Canadian Product Monograph for Zepbound similarly contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma and warns of gastrointestinal adverse reactions and the potential for drug interactions due to delayed gastric emptying.[38]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:406:  25: Limitations: The corpus is heavily weighted towards lower-tier evidence, with only 14% of sources being T1 primary studies and 32% being T4 narrative reviews. The pipeline detected 12 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight and weight loss, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcomes. The evidence spans from 2010 to the present, but the concentration of lower-tier sources may limit the robustness of the synthesized conclusions.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:3566:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the trade name Mounjaro in 2022 as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24][25] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The FDA label carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, based on findings in rats, and contraindicates tirzepatide in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2.[26] The FDA later approved tirzepatide for chronic weight management under the trade name Zepbound in 2023, indicated as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for adults with obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥27 kg/m²) with at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27][28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for Mounjaro valid throughout the EU on 15 September 2022.[29] The EMA's approved indication for type 2 diabetes is for the treatment of adults, adolescents, and children aged 10 years and above with insufficiently controlled disease as an adjunct to diet and exercise, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic products.[30] The EMA also approved an indication for weight management, defined as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31][30][32] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific problems associated with obesity, or a BMI below 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE's recommendation is contingent upon a commercial arrangement that makes tirzepatide available to the NHS at a cost-effective price.[33][34] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide as an option for people with a BMI of more than 35 and at least one weight-related illness, with an initial rollout prioritized for those in specialist services.[35] Key labeling warnings and precautions are consistent across authorities and include risks of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, hypersensitivity reactions, and hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin or insulin secretagogues.[36][26][30] The FDA and EMA labels also warn that tirzepatide delays gastric emptying, which can impact the absorption of concomitantly administered oral medications, necessitating guidance for patients using oral contraceptives.[25][37][30] The Canadian Product Monograph for Zepbound similarly contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma and warns of gastrointestinal adverse reactions and the potential for drug interactions due to delayed gastric emptying.[38]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:276:  "question": "What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:295:  "run_id": "SWEEP_clinical_clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm_1776634472",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:296:  "slug": "clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:302:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:306:In the SURPASS-1 trial of patients with type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent reductions in HbA1c and body weight.[1] In SURPASS-3, tirzepatide was compared with insulin degludec as an add-on to metformin with or without SGLT2 inhibitors.[2] The SURPASS-4 trial compared tirzepatide versus insulin glargine in patients with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk.[3] In Japanese populations, tirzepatide monotherapy was evaluated against dulaglutide in SURPASS J-mono, and as an add-on to oral medication in SURPASS J-combo.[4][5] In Chinese patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin, the SURPASS-CN-INS trial assessed tirzepatide added to basal insulin.[6] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, SURMOUNT-4 showed that after a 36-week lead-in with tirzepatide resulting in 20.9% weight reduction, continued treatment led to an additional 5.5% weight loss over 52 weeks, whereas switching to placebo led to 14.0% weight regain.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:310:An adjusted indirect treatment comparison of tirzepatide versus semaglutide 2 mg in patients with type 2 diabetes found that tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg significantly reduced HbA1c versus semaglutide 2 mg, with estimated treatment differences of −0.36% and −0.4%, respectively, and significantly reduced body weight with differences of −3.15 kg and −5.15 kg, respectively.[8] Another indirect comparison in people with obesity or overweight and type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg, with mean differences of 2.57% and 4.79%, respectively, and greater HbA1c reductions.[9] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, a direct head-to-head comparison showed a least-squares mean percent change in weight of −20.2% with tirzepatide versus −13.7% with semaglutide.[10] For cardiovascular outcomes in patients with diabetes and cardiovascular disease, tirzepatide was compared with dulaglutide, where a 6-component cardiorenal end point occurred in 23.7% of tirzepatide-treated patients versus 27.4% of dulaglutide-treated patients.[11] One systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a pooled standardized mean difference of 0.75.[12]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:314:In randomized controlled trials, the overall safety profile of tirzepatide is similar to that of GLP-1 receptor agonists, primarily characterized by gastrointestinal adverse events.[13] A systematic review of RCTs found the incidences of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea were higher than with placebo but consistent with GLP-1 receptor agonists.[13] Regarding hypoglycemia, one systematic review reported tirzepatide 15 mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI 1.19–12.30) in a pooled analysis of trials.[13] A separate network meta-analysis in patients with obesity or overweight found no significant difference in hypoglycemia events between tirzepatide and GLP-1 receptor agonists but noted tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk (<54 mg/dL).[14] The FAERS analysis reported tirzepatide was associated with a similar risk of diabetic retinopathy and medullary thyroid cancer versus SGLT-2 inhibitors.[15] Despite a black box warning for thyroid C-cell tumors, one retrospective cohort study found patients treated with tirzepatide had a significantly lower incidence of malignant thyroid cancer compared to controls (Relative Risk 0.348, p < 0.001).[16]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:318:Meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials demonstrate a dose-dependent relationship for tirzepatide's effects on HbA1c and body weight in type 2 diabetes.[17] Another meta-analysis confirmed these trends, finding HbA1c reductions of 0.20% with 10 mg and 0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and weight reductions of 2.64 kg and 4.26 kg, respectively.[18] A more recent network meta-analysis also found significant reductions in HbA1c with tirzepatide 10 mg (MD: -0.19) and 15 mg (MD: -0.32) versus the 5 mg dose, alongside weight reductions of 1.96 kg and 3.31 kg, respectively.[19] Regarding the time to achieve clinical thresholds, a pre-planned exploratory analysis of the SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 trials found the median time to first achieve a body weight loss of ≥5% was faster with tirzepatide 5 mg (16.0 weeks) and with the 10 and 15 mg doses (12.4 weeks) than with the active comparator semaglutide 1 mg (24.0 weeks).[20] The same analysis reported the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for each tirzepatide dose.[20] In terms of safety, meta-analyses indicate the risk of total adverse events and gastrointestinal adverse events is higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[17][18] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy, but the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[17]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:322:In older patients with type 2 diabetes and diminished β-cell function, a case report suggests tirzepatide may help control postprandial hyperglycemia but may not replace basal insulin for fasting control, requiring monitoring for sarcopenia.[21] In a prespecified analysis of the SUMMIT trial in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide's effect on cardiovascular outcomes was consistent regardless of diabetes status, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 (95% CI: 0.35-1.15) for the composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure in patients with type 2 diabetes and 0.61 (95% CI: 0.33-1.10) in those without diabetes (Pinteraction = 0.95).[22] However, weight loss with tirzepatide was less pronounced in patients with type 2 diabetes (10.4% [95% CI: 8.7%-12.2%]) compared to those without diabetes (12.9% [95% CI: 11.2%-14.6%]) (Pinteraction = 0.04).[22] Weight loss was significantly greater in patients with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[23] A separate post-hoc analysis of SURPASS trials indicated that tirzepatide was effective in reducing HbA1c and body weight in patients with glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies (GADA-positive), though the HbA1c reduction was slightly less compared to GADA-negative patients (estimated treatment difference 0.21% [95% CI: 0.03, 0.39]; p=0.024).[24]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:326:Limitations: The corpus has significant gaps in its tier distribution, with only 18% of sources being T1 primary studies and a substantial 28% classified as T4 preprints. The pipeline detected 12 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight, where sources disagree dramatically on the magnitude of the reported effect across different dosages. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the high volume of lower-tier evidence and preprints indicates a reliance on less mature research.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:585:outputs/full_scale_v7/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm\report.md:17:Meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials demonstrate a dose-dependent relationship for tirzepatide's effects on HbA1c and body weight in type 2 diabetes.[17] Another meta-analysis confirmed these trends, finding HbA1c reductions of 0.20% with 10 mg and 0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and weight reductions of 2.64 kg and 4.26 kg, respectively.[18] A more recent network meta-analysis also found significant reductions in HbA1c with tirzepatide 10 mg (MD: -0.19) and 15 mg (MD: -0.32) versus the 5 mg dose, alongside weight reductions of 1.96 kg and 3.31 kg, respectively.[19] Regarding the time to achieve clinical thresholds, a pre-planned exploratory analysis of the SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 trials found the median time to first achieve a body weight loss of ≥5% was faster with tirzepatide 5 mg (16.0 weeks) and with the 10 and 15 mg doses (12.4 weeks) than with the active comparator semaglutide 1 mg (24.0 weeks).[20] The same analysis reported the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for each tirzepatide dose.[20] In terms of safety, meta-analyses indicate the risk of total adverse events and gastrointestinal adverse events is higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[17][18] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy, but the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[17]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:702:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command 'Get-Content -Raw outputs/full_scale_v7/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/verification_details.json' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:704:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:708:Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:712:In a direct head-to-head trial in patients with obesity without diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss compared to semaglutide, with a least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 of −20.2% versus −13.7%.[5] Participants were also more likely to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% with tirzepatide.[5] Another phase 3 trial reported a mean percentage change in body weight with tirzepatide of −20.2% compared with −13.7% for semaglutide at 72 weeks.[6] A real-world retrospective cohort study in patients without diabetes also found greater 6-month mean percentage weight reduction with tirzepatide (−11.15%) versus semaglutide (−8.83%).[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a greater standardized mean difference for weight loss.[8] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, a network meta-analysis found all tirzepatide doses (5, 10, 15 mg) were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c.[9] An indirect treatment comparison also concluded tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg in people with obesity and type 2 diabetes.[10] Compared to insulin, tirzepatide showed greater efficacy; a meta-analysis found once-weekly tirzepatide significantly decreased HbA1c and body weight compared with daily insulin glargine and degludec, with a lower risk of hypoglycemia.[11] Specifically, in a trial adding therapy to basal insulin, the mean change in HbA1c at week 52 was −2.1% with tirzepatide versus −1.1% with insulin lispro, with less hypoglycemia and more body weight reduction.[12] Another meta-analysis reported all three tirzepatide doses resulted in a significant reduction in HbA1c and a significant decrease in body weight compared with long-acting insulin.[13] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal events were the most common adverse events for both tirzepatide and semaglutide in the head-to-head obesity trial, with one source reporting at least one adverse event in approximately 76.7% of patients on tirzepatide and 79% on semaglutide.[5][6] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide showed a statistically significant, higher likelihood of experiencing nausea compared with placebo or exenatide, but no significant difference in treatment discontinuation due to adverse events compared to several GLP-1 receptor agonists.[14]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:716:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide and are dose-dependent, with reported incidences of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[15] Among gastrointestinal events, nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent at any dose.[15] Drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%.[15] Regarding hypoglycemia, a meta-analysis found patients treated with tirzepatide had no significantly increased risk compared with placebo overall (OR= 2.05, 95%CI:0.95 to 4.42, p=0.07).[16] Another analysis reported tirzepatide 15mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI [1.19- 12.30], P=0.02).[17] Conversely, a different study in patients achieving normoglycemia found low rates, with 98.5% not experiencing level 2 or 3 severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) and 91% not experiencing level 1 hypoglycemia (≤70 mg/dL).[18] Rates of fatal adverse events, severe hypoglycemia, acute pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, and cholecystitis are reported as extremely low (≤ 1%) across all doses.[15] An analysis of FDA adverse event reports suggested an association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (reporting odds ratio = 2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), though this was lower than associations for other GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:720:The effects of tirzepatide on glycemic and weight outcomes are dose-dependent within the 5 mg to 15 mg range.[20] This analysis also noted that fasting serum glucose showed improvement with the 15 mg dose.[21] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg dose range, the effect in lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy benefit.[20] A separate meta-analysis confirmed that the 15 mg dose provided the greatest reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the 10 mg and 5 mg doses.[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events increased with higher doses, though one analysis reported this increase was without statistical significance.[21] Another analysis found the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[22]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:724:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:728:Limitations: The corpus exhibits significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 15% of sources being T1 primary studies and a heavy reliance on T7 sources at 32%. The pipeline detected 11 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcome. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, which may not capture earlier foundational research.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:528:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:532:In the same trial, tirzepatide also induced significantly greater weight loss, with reductions of −7.8 kg, −10.3 kg, and −12.4 kg for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively, compared to −6.2 kg with semaglutide 1 mg.[1] Weight loss in SURPASS-3 was also substantial, with changes of −7.5 kg, −10.7 kg, and −12.9 kg for the three tirzepatide doses, respectively, versus a weight gain of 2.3 kg with insulin degludec.[1] A pre-planned exploratory analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found that the median time to achieve an HbA1c <7.0% was faster with tirzepatide (8.1 weeks across doses) than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[2] At the same 104-week timepoint in SURPASS-4, body weight reductions were −7.6 kg, −10.0 kg, and −11.4 kg for the tirzepatide doses, versus a gain of 2.1 kg with insulin glargine.[3] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in an Asia-Pacific population, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction at 40 weeks, with least squares mean reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% with insulin glargine.[4] The same trial reported superior body weight reductions with tirzepatide: −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the three doses, respectively, compared to a gain of 1.5 kg with insulin glargine.[4] A post-hoc subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found that reductions in HbA1c and body weight with tirzepatide were generally consistent across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline BMI, and diabetes duration.[5] The corresponding weight reductions in SURPASS J-mono were −5.8 kg, −8.5 kg, and −10.7 kg for tirzepatide versus −0.5 kg with dulaglutide.[1]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:536:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common tolerability concern with tirzepatide and are typically mild to moderate in severity.[6] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials reported that gastrointestinal adverse events were the most frequently reported category in tirzepatide-related individual case safety reports in a real-world pharmacovigilance database, with pancreatitis and vomiting being the most common specific events.[7] For vomiting, incidence with tirzepatide 15 mg ranged from 6% to 18.1%, and for diarrhea, it ranged from 12% to 31%.[1] One systematic review found that compared to 5 mg, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea, and 15 mg led to more diarrhea than 5 mg, suggesting some dose-dependence for specific gastrointestinal events.[8] The risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide is generally low when not used with insulin or insulin secretagogues.[9] Regarding thyroid safety, real-world pharmacovigilance data has detected a disproportionate reporting signal for medullary thyroid cancer with tirzepatide (ROR 13.67, 95% CI 4.35–42.96).[10] This aligns with a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors seen in rodents, and it is unknown whether tirzepatide causes such tumors in humans, including medullary thyroid carcinoma.[11] However, the same real-world analysis noted tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal trials in the SURPASS program did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[12] Discontinuation due to adverse events appears dose-related; one systematic review found the risk of discontinuation by adverse event was significantly higher with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75, 95%CI[1.16-2.63]) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03, 95%CI [1.37-3.01]) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:540:An adjusted indirect treatment comparison (aITC) suggests tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg also provide superior glycemic and weight control compared to the higher semaglutide 2 mg dose approved for diabetes, with estimated differences in HbA1c of −0.36% and −0.4% and in body weight of −3.15 kg and −5.15 kg, respectively.[13] A systematic review and network meta-analysis of RCTs concluded that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, while tirzepatide 15 mg, 10 mg, and 5 mg demonstrated greater weight loss efficacy than semaglutide 2.0 mg, 1.0 mg, and 0.5 mg, respectively.[14] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets; for example, 57% of participants on tirzepatide 15 mg met three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7%, systolic BP <130 mmHg, LDL-cholesterol <100 mg/dL, and weight loss >10%) compared to 34% on semaglutide.[15] Compared to basal insulin, a meta-analysis of SURPASS trials showed tirzepatide provides superior HbA1c reduction (mean differences ranging from −0.89% to −1.23% across doses) and profound weight loss benefits (mean differences from −8.41 kg to −12.49 kg) versus weight gain with insulin.[16] In a network meta-analysis of patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg showed statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus the GLP-1 RA dulaglutide 1.5 mg.[17] Regarding cardiovascular outcomes, the SURPASS-CVOT trial found tirzepatide was noninferior to dulaglutide for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), with a hazard ratio of 0.92 (95.3% CI, 0.83 to 1.01).[18] A real-world trial emulation study reported a hazard ratio of 1.06 (95% CI, 0.95 to 1.18) for MACE including mortality in a head-to-head comparison of tirzepatide versus semaglutide, suggesting comparable cardiovascular benefit.[19] The aforementioned aITC reported the overall safety profile was similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 2 mg.[13]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:544:This dose-response relationship for HbA1c reduction was consistent across trials; in SURPASS-4 at 104 weeks, mean reductions were -2.3%, -2.5%, and -2.6% for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[3] Weight loss also followed a clear dose-dependent pattern, with greater reductions at higher doses.[20] In SURPASS-2, body weight reductions were -7.8 kg, -10.3 kg, and -12.4 kg for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, a trend replicated in SURPASS-4 with reductions of -7.6 kg, -10.0 kg, and -11.4 kg at 104 weeks.[21][3] A meta-analysis confirmed that compared to the 5 mg dose, the 10 mg and 15 mg doses provided significantly greater weight loss of -2.64 kg and -4.26 kg, respectively.[22] The proportion of patients achieving weight loss thresholds also increased with dose; in SURPASS-2, 65%, 76%, and 80% of patients achieved ≥5% weight loss on 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, while ≥15% weight loss was achieved by 15%, 24%, and 36%.[1] An adjusted indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater HbA1c and body weight reductions compared to semaglutide 2 mg, while the 5 mg dose showed similar efficacy to semaglutide 2 mg.[13] The time to achieve glycemic and weight loss targets was faster with all tirzepatide doses compared to active controls, with the median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% being 8.1 weeks for each tirzepatide dose versus 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg.[2] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events were common and showed a dose-dependent increase in incidence.[20] A meta-analysis reported that the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[22] One meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, glycemic and weight efficacy is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose providing the greatest efficacy benefit, while the 5 mg dose may offer a more favorable safety profile.[20]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:548:A post-hoc subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found that the efficacy of tirzepatide was generally consistent across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, and diabetes duration, though some differences were observed.[5] Patients with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater weight loss across all tirzepatide doses compared to those weighing <75 kg.[5] A post-hoc analysis of pooled SURPASS-1 to -5 data reported that tirzepatide's effect on HbA1c reduction was consistent between participants aged <65 and ≥65 years.[23] In a small observational pilot study of older Japanese patients (mean age 79.5 years), tirzepatide initiation improved time in range from 53.2% to 78.9% as measured by continuous glucose monitoring.[24] For patients with high cardiovascular risk, the SURPASS-4 trial specifically enrolled individuals with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk, demonstrating superior HbA1c and weight reductions with tirzepatide versus insulin glargine.[25] The subsequent SURPASS-CVOT trial, which enrolled patients with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, found tirzepatide was non-inferior to dulaglutide for the primary composite cardiovascular endpoint (hazard ratio 0.92; 95% CI 0.83 to 1.01).[18] A real-world trial emulation analysis benchmarking against SURPASS-CVOT estimated a hazard ratio of 0.87 (95% CI 0.75 to 1.01) for a composite outcome including mortality for tirzepatide versus dulaglutide in an expanded population.[19] The safety profile, characterized predominantly by gastrointestinal adverse events, was generally consistent across these populations and trials.[4][26][5][18]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:552:Limitations: The corpus is dominated by lower-tier sources, with only 18% of sources classified as T1 primary studies. High-severity contradictions were detected on the effect of tirzepatide on body weight and weight loss, with sources disagreeing on the magnitude of the effect by several hundred to several thousand percent. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the low proportion of high-tier evidence limits the strength of conclusions.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_5\codex_stderr.log:457:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_5\codex_stderr.log:461:In the SURPASS-2 trial, tirzepatide demonstrated superior glycemic and weight reduction compared to the GLP-1 receptor agonist semaglutide 1 mg in adults with type 2 diabetes on metformin.[1] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found that all tirzepatide doses increased the odds of achieving composite therapeutic targets for HbA1c, blood pressure, and weight loss compared to semaglutide, with 57% of participants on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets versus 34% on semaglutide.[1] Across the SURPASS trials, the proportion of participants achieving an HbA1c <7.0% ranged from 81% to 97%, and those achieving HbA1c ≤6.5% ranged from 66% to 95% with tirzepatide.[2] A systematic review notes that across phase 3 trials, dual GLP-1/GIP agonists like tirzepatide achieved greater reductions in HbA1c (up to 2.4%) and body weight (up to 22%) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[3] An individual participant data meta-analysis of phase 3 trials reported that tirzepatide 15 mg/week significantly reduced the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) compared to comparators, with a hazard ratio of 0.67 (95% CI, 0.51-0.87).[4] Furthermore, improvements in cardiometabolic components like weight, waist circumference, diastolic blood pressure, and fasting blood glucose during tirzepatide therapy were associated with a lower hazard of MACE.[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_5\codex_stderr.log:465:In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, the most common adverse events with tirzepatide were mild to moderate decreased appetite, diarrhea, and nausea, with no severe hypoglycemia reported.[5] In SURPASS-3, any adverse event rates were 61–73% for tirzepatide versus 54% for insulin degludec, and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events was 7–11% versus 1%.[6] A review notes that adverse effects are similar to those observed with GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy, with gastrointestinal effects including nausea (20–33%), diarrhea (18–22%), and vomiting (8–13%) being the most common, and are mostly mild to moderate in severity during dose escalation.[7] In SURPASS-1, the rates of any adverse event for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg were 69%, 67%, and 64%, respectively, versus 66% for placebo, with serious adverse event rates of 4%, 2%, and 1% versus 3%.[6] Treatment discontinuation due to adverse events in that trial was 3%, 5%, and 7% for the respective tirzepatide doses versus 3% for placebo.[6]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_5\codex_stderr.log:469:A meta-analysis of trials in patients with obesity or type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide induced a mean weight reduction of 10.39 kg versus placebo (95% CI: –10.80 to –9.99).[8] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, SURMOUNT-5, a phase 3 trial comparing tirzepatide 15 mg to semaglutide 2.4 mg, the mean percentage change in body weight was a reduction of 20.2% with tirzepatide compared with 13.7% for semaglutide.[9] That same trial reported patients treated with tirzepatide were 1.3, 1.6, 1.8, and 2 times more likely to have at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% weight loss, respectively, compared to semaglutide.[9] A cost-effectiveness analysis based on the SURPASS-2 trial in patients with type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide had a lower cost per responder than semaglutide 1 mg for achieving HbA1c < 5.7% (all doses) and weight loss ≥10% (all doses).[10] The number needed to treat (NNT) for all doses of tirzepatide were statistically significantly lower than that for semaglutide 1 mg to achieve weight loss endpoints of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%.[10] In that substudy, tirzepatide resulted in a 33.9% reduction in fat mass versus 8.2% with placebo and a 10.9% reduction in lean mass versus 2.6% with placebo at week 72.[11] A post-hoc analysis of the SURMOUNT trials reported that treatment-emergent adverse events potentially related to macronutrient malnutrition, such as abnormal loss of weight and underweight, were uncommon, occurring in 0.12% of tirzepatide-treated participants.[12] The same analysis found 0.38% of tirzepatide-treated participants reached a BMI < 18.5 kg/m².[12] For safety comparisons, the meta-analysis reported tirzepatide was associated with increased risks of nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting compared to placebo, while aggregated data indicated no increased risk of headache (OR=1.00, 95%CI:0.84 to 1.20).[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_5\codex_stderr.log:473:Limitations: The corpus is heavily weighted towards lower-tier sources, with only 19% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 41% as T4 narrative reviews. The pipeline detected 13 high-severity contradictions, most notably where sources disagree on the magnitude of tirzepatide's effect on body weight and weight loss across multiple dosage levels. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the low proportion of primary research may limit the robustness of the synthesized conclusions.

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_8\findings.md:13:  However, the gate is narrower than the stated safety goal for primary papers with diagnostic titles. It only recognizes article-type diagnostic titles, not primary-study diagnostic titles. Fresh classify_source_tier() integration scenarios showed that "Randomized placebo-controlled trial of tirzepatide" + body GUIDELINE and "SURPASS-9 trial: tirzepatide in adults with obesity" + body GUIDELINE both return T4 via R8b_body_guideline. That is a material over-demotion path for exactly the false-positive body-guideline leakage Pass 7 exposed. It is documented in the tests, but documenting the behavior does not make it architecturally safe.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_8\findings.md:15:  I also found one under-demotion / over-grant risk in the advisory path: "Case report: Pancreatitis with tirzepatide" + false SR_MA body signal is advisory, then R9 classifies it as T4 through the narrative-review detector because "report" contains "review" as a substring. The tested case "Pancreatitis with tirzepatide: a case report" only asserts advisory_only and does not assert tier. This is not caused solely by M-17f, but the advisory branch exposes reliance on imperfect title rules after suppressing the body signal.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:134:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command 'Get-Content -Raw outputs/full_scale_v18/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:136:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:140:This trial also reported that 82% to 86% of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved an HbA1c of less than 7.0% at 40 weeks, compared to 79% with semaglutide.[1] In the SURPASS-3 trial, a 52-week, open-label, active-controlled study comparing tirzepatide to insulin degludec in patients on metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor, HbA1c reductions were −1.93%, −2.20%, and −2.37% for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.34% with insulin degludec.[2][3] A pre-planned exploratory analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found that the median time to achieve an HbA1c of less than 7.0% was faster with tirzepatide (8.1 weeks for each dose) than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[4] In the SURPASS-4 trial, which enrolled patients with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg reduced HbA1c by −2.24%, −2.43%, and −2.58%, respectively, over 52 weeks, compared to a reduction of −1.44% with insulin glargine.[2][5] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial conducted in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine, with HbA1c reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% with insulin glargine at 40 weeks.[2][6]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:144:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common safety concern with tirzepatide, occurring in a dose-dependent manner.[7] Another systematic review noted that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea (P<0.001) and vomiting (P=0.007 for 5 mg vs. 10 mg; P<0.001 for 5 mg vs. 15 mg).[8][9] These events often led to treatment discontinuation; one analysis found drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%, and another systematic review reported higher discontinuation rates with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[7][8][9] In the obesity trial SURMOUNT-4, during the initial open-label lead-in period with tirzepatide, 35.5% of participants experienced nausea, 21.1% diarrhea, 20.7% constipation, and 16.3% vomiting.[10][11] In SURPASS-AP-Combo, where tirzepatide was compared to insulin glargine, no severe hypoglycemia was reported.[6] A meta-analysis found the incidence of mild hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) was highest with the tirzepatide 10 mg dose at 22.6% (95% CI, 9.2%-39.8%).[7] However, another systematic review concluded the overall safety profile was similar to GLP-1 receptor agonists except for a higher risk of hypoglycemia with the 15 mg dose (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI 1.19-12.30) and higher discontinuation rates.[8][9] A meta-analysis found rates of MTC and other serious events like acute pancreatitis and cholecystitis were ≤1% across all tirzepatide doses.[7] In the SURMOUNT-CN obesity trial, one case of papillary thyroid cancer occurred in a participant with a history of thyroid nodules receiving tirzepatide 15 mg, which was considered unrelated to treatment by the investigator, and no cases of medullary thyroid cancer were reported.[12] Similarly, in the SURMOUNT-4 trial, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma were reported.[10][11] A real-world pharmacovigilance analysis identified a signal for medullary thyroid carcinoma with an EB05 of 3.55, based on 3 reported cases.[13] Other adverse events of interest include pancreatitis, with the same pharmacovigilance analysis reporting 190 cases and a signal strength (EB05=6.85), though adjudicated confirmed cases in trials were low; for example, SURMOUNT-4 reported no adjudication-confirmed cases of pancreatitis.[13][10][11] Gallbladder-related events such as cholelithiasis and acute cholecystitis have also been reported at low frequencies.[10][11][12][7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:148:A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide concluded that all three tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, while demonstrating greater weight loss than the corresponding semaglutide doses.[14] A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving composite therapeutic targets, such as HbA1c <7.0% with weight loss >10%, compared to semaglutide 1 mg.[15] For weight loss specifically in populations with overweight or obesity, a meta-analysis of direct comparative studies reported a standardized mean difference of 0.75 favoring tirzepatide over semaglutide, and a real-world evidence study in a US cohort with overweight or obesity reported patients on tirzepatide had significantly higher rates of achieving ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15% weight loss within one year.[16][17] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and body weight reductions ranging from -7.5 kg to -12.9 kg, whereas insulin degludec was associated with weight gain of +2.3 kg.[3] Similarly, in SURPASS-4 versus insulin glargine in patients at increased cardiovascular risk, all tirzepatide doses provided superior HbA1c reductions and weight reductions between -6.4 kg and -10.6 kg, compared to weight gain of +1.9 kg with insulin glargine.[5] Another systematic review and meta-analysis confirmed these findings, reporting tirzepatide was associated with significantly greater proportions of patients achieving HbA1c targets of <7.0% (80-89% vs. 49%) and weight loss ≥5% (62-83% vs. 7%) compared to long-acting insulin.[18] A network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The indirect comparison versus semaglutide 2 mg reported a similar overall safety profile, though tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg had higher odds of treatment discontinuation due to adverse events.[20] Compared to basal insulin, tirzepatide is associated with more gastrointestinal adverse events but a significantly lower risk of hypoglycemia.[21][18]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:152:Tirzepatide demonstrates dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight reduction across its 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses in patients with type 2 diabetes.[2][22][23] Similarly, for weight loss, the same meta-analysis found that compared to tirzepatide 5 mg, the 10 mg dose reduced body weight by an additional 2.64 kg and the 15 mg dose by an additional 4.26 kg.[23] This pattern is evident in individual trials; in SURPASS-3, weight reductions at 52 weeks were −7.5 kg, −10.7 kg, and −12.9 kg for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively, compared to a 2.3 kg increase with insulin degludec.[2][3] In SURPASS-4, weight changes were −7.1 kg, −9.5 kg, and −11.7 kg for the three doses versus a 1.9 kg increase with insulin glargine.[2][5] The proportion of patients achieving clinically meaningful weight loss thresholds also increases with dose.[2] The time to achieve treatment targets is also dose-influenced, with higher doses leading to faster responses.[4] A pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found that the median time to first achieve an HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all tirzepatide doses, compared to 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec.[4] For weight loss, the median time to achieve ≥5% body weight reduction in SURPASS-2 was 16.0 weeks with tirzepatide 5 mg and 12.4 weeks with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses, versus 24.0 weeks with semaglutide 1 mg.[4] While efficacy increases with dose, so does the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events, though serious adverse event rates are generally similar across doses.[22][23] One meta-analysis reports that the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with tirzepatide 10 mg (risk ratio 1.20) and 15 mg (risk ratio 1.26) compared to the 5 mg dose.[23] Another analysis concluded that tirzepatide 5 mg had the best safety profile among the three doses, while the 15 mg dose provided the greatest efficacy benefit for glycemic and weight control.[22] For optimal long-term weight maintenance, continued treatment with the maximum tolerated dose (10 mg or 15 mg) is necessary, as evidenced by the SURMOUNT-4 trial where switching to placebo after 36 weeks of tirzepatide led to a 14.0% weight regain, while continued treatment yielded an additional 5.5% reduction.[11]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:156:Tirzepatide is approved by the FDA under the proprietary name Mounjaro as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg subcutaneously once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) authorizes tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for the same T2DM indication in adults, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other antidiabetic medicines.[26][27] The EMA also approves tirzepatide for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[28][26] For the chronic weight management indication, the FDA approved tirzepatide under the name Zepbound, with an identical starting and titration schedule up to 15 mg once weekly.[29][30] A key safety warning from both agencies is a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, as tirzepatide caused thyroid C-cell tumors in rats, though human relevance is unknown.[31][32] Consequently, tirzepatide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[31][32] Other warnings and precautions common to the labels include the risk of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin secretagogues or insulin, hypersensitivity reactions, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, and potential impact on absorption of oral medications due to delayed gastric emptying.[31][32] The EMA product information additionally notes a mean increase in heart rate and includes diabetic retinopathy complications and pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia as precautions.[26] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating T2DM in adults only under specific conditions: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI under 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE guidance also specifies using lower BMI thresholds for people from certain ethnic backgrounds.[33] For weight management, NICE provides a prescribing guide, confirming the starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly and titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly.[35]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:160:Limitations: The evidence base is heavily weighted towards lower-tier sources, with only 14% of sources being T1 primary studies and 33% classified as T4 narrative reviews. The pipeline detected 13 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight and weight loss, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcomes. The analyzed evidence spans from 2010 to the present, which may not capture the full historical context of earlier therapeutic approaches. The low proportion of high-tier evidence (T1-T3 combined at 39%) suggests conclusions are often supported by secondary or summary literature.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:2610:comparison was favorable: severe hypoglycemia occurred in 0.4% of tirzepatide-treated patients versus

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3032:Structural pattern: V17 is organized by topic class: Efficacy, Safety, Comparative, Dose Response, Population Subgroups, then Methods, Contradiction disclosures, and Bibliography. It is not organized around primary studies. Named trials appear inside broad paragraphs, often as supporting examples rather than as the governing units of analysis. The Efficacy section opens mid-referent with "That same trial" (line 5), which makes the local paragraph hard to read without prior hidden context. The report also imports obesity-without-diabetes SURMOUNT material into a type 2 diabetes question (lines 5 and 9), which broadens coverage but weakens direct answer focus.
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3034:Claim architecture: A typical V17 claim is compressed into: source type or trial name -> broad conclusion -> citation. It often omits design, N, baseline, endpoint, dose-specific absolute result, comparator result, confidence interval, and p-value in the same sentence. Example: "In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide also demonstrated superior glycemic control and weight loss..." (line 5). That is directionally correct, but it does not carry the trial skeleton that lets a reader judge magnitude or applicability.
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3076:Contradiction handling: Gemini handles some source differences poorly. It reports SURPASS-CVOT noninferiority and P=0.09 for superiority (lines 428-433), but later concludes that cardiovascular and renal protection were "definitively proven" and that tirzepatide "outperform[ed]" active cardioprotective comparators (lines 664-669). That is an internal contradiction: the reported endpoint supports noninferiority with numerical trends, not definitive cardiovascular superiority.

.\outputs\codex_findings\m29_code_audit\findings.md:32:The rule block is domain-agnostic in the implementation reviewed. .\outputs\codex_findings\m29_code_audit\findings.md:36:The rule is not limited to the V18 tirzepatide defect. It explicitly covers regulatory, standards-setting, governance, countries, agencies, courts, and rulemaking bodies. That should help cross-domain prompts such as FDA/EMA policy synthesis on AI-enabled medical devices and SEC/FTC due-diligence synthesis, because the same failure mode is unsupported equivalence across authorities. The clinical boxed-warning example is specific in shape, but not hard-coded to a named clinical product or regulator.
