.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:40:9. Legit guideline title with false SR/MA body: `Clinical practice guideline for tirzepatide in obesity`, body `SR_MA`, journal article. Gate logs `article-type`; R9 returns `T4` via guideline/explainer title. Pass.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:41:10. Primary trial title plus false guideline body: `Randomized trial of tirzepatide in adults with obesity`, body `GUIDELINE`, journal article. Gate logs `primary-study`; R9 returns `T1`. Pass.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:47:With a primary-trial title that triggers M-17g, `Randomized trial of tirzepatide in adults with obesity` plus that false body signal classifies `T1` through `R9_openalex_primary_study`, with body advisory. This is the architectural win.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:51:With an explicit guideline title, `Clinical practice guideline for tirzepatide` plus false body `SR_MA` logs body advisory and classifies `T4` through `R9_openalex_guideline_explainer`. This fixes the SR/MA false-demotion case for legitimate guidelines.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:53:One non-R8b nuance: a title like `Randomized trial of tirzepatide after previous guidelines recommend lifestyle intervention` triggers the primary-study advisory gate, but R9 later demotes to `T4` because `_detect_guideline_or_explainer_title` matches `guidelines`. That is a separate title-heuristic interaction, not a failure of the R8b union gate.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:57:One residual over-protection risk remains. The primary-study marker `effect of` / `effects of` is broad. In the scenario `Effect of tirzepatide on beta-cell function` with a legitimate `CASE_REPORT` body signal and OpenAlex journal article metadata, R8b suppresses the body demotion and R9 returns `T1`. Most legitimate case reports, systematic reviews, and guidelines should carry title labels caught by the article-type arm or R9 title demoters, so this is non-blocking but should be monitored.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_9\findings.md:61:One residual under-protection risk remains by design: primary journal papers with bare titles and no positive primary marker are not protected from false body signals. Example: a true primary paper titled only `Tirzepatide in type 2 diabetes` would not satisfy the primary-study arm; if the detector emits `GUIDELINE`, R8b still demotes to `T4`. This is the cost of keeping the gate conservative and avoiding over-trusting OpenAlex article+journal alone.

If you only add missing patterns without reordering milestones, progress will regress.

.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_015.txt:1:Treatment with tirzepatide led to significant weight loss in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities (excluding type 2 diabetes), according to results from the phase 3 SURMOUNT-3 and SURMOUNT-4 studies. Tirzepatide is an investigational once-weekly, dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist that integrates the actions of both incretins into a single molecule. The multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel, placebo-controlled SURMOUNT-3 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04657016](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04657016?term=NCT04657016&rank=1)) evaluated the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for 72 weeks after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention lead-in period in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities, excluding type 2 diabetes. At the end of the 12-week period, study participants (N=579) achieved 6.9% mean weight loss and were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive tirzepatide or placebo subcutaneously once weekly. The efficacy estimand (efficacy prior to discontinuation of study drug) results demonstrated that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 21.1% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 3.3% weight regain for the placebo group. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. The total mean body weight re
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_015.txt:5:ide for 72 weeks after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention lead-in period in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities, excluding type 2 diabetes. At the end of the 12-week period, study participants (N=579) achieved 6.9% mean weight loss and were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive tirzepatide or placebo subcutaneously once weekly. The efficacy estimand (efficacy prior to discontinuation of study drug) results demonstrated that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 21.1% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 3.3% weight regain for the placebo group. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. The total mean body weight reduction for patients treated with tirzepatide from study entry after 12 weeks of lifestyle intervention to 72 weeks of treatment was 26.6%. The treatment-regimen estimand (estimated average treatment effect regardless of treatment discontinuation) results demonstrated that tirzepatide was associated with an additional 18.4% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 2.5% regain with placebo. Moreover, 87.5% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 16.5% of those in the placebo arm. The total mean body weight reduction for patients treated with tirzepatide from study entry after 12 weeks of lifestyle intervention to 72 weeks of treatment was 24.3%. The multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel, placebo-controlled SURMOUNT-4 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04660643](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04660643?term=NCT04660643&rank=1)) evaluated the efficacy and safety of tirz
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_015.txt:9:riod in which all participants received tirzepatide, patients (N=670) were then randomly assigned 1:1 to continue to receive tirzepatide or switch to placebo for 52 weeks. At the end of the 36-week tirzepatide lead-in period, these patients achieved 21.1% mean weight loss. The efficacy estimand results showed that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 6.7% reduction in body weight from randomization at 36 weeks to 88 weeks vs a 14.8% weight regain with placebo. The total mean weight reduction for patients who remained on tirzepatide over the entire 88-week period was 26.0%. Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes Published June 25, 2021 N Engl J Med 2021;385:503-515 DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2107519 Abstract Background Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that is under development for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. The efficacy and safety of once-weekly tirzepatide as compared with semaglutide, a selective GLP-1 receptor agonist, are unknown. Methods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was −2.01 percentage points, −2.24 percentage points, and −2.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_006.txt:5:Methods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was −2.01 percentage points, −2.24 percentage points, and −2.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-mg tirzepatide groups and the semaglutide group were −0.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], −0.28 to −0.03; P=0.02), −0.39 percentage points (95% CI, −0.51 to −0.26; P 8.5% [≤69 or >69 mmol per mol]). Tirzepatide was initiated at a dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, and the doses were increased by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks until the randomly assigned dose was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial. Semaglutide was initiated at a dose of 0.25 mg once weekly, and the dose was doubled every 4 weeks until 1 mg was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial.20 Dose de-escalation was not allowed. The initiation of new antihyperglycemic medications was allowed ac

One immediate event was reported in the tirzepatide 15-mg group. ¶ Diabetic retinopathy was confirmed by funduscopic examination. Glycemic End Points At 40 weeks, reductions in the mean glycated hemoglobin level with tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg were −2.01 percentage points, −2.24 percentage points, and −2.30 percentage points, respectively, as compared with −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide (Figure 1A). All tirzepatide doses were found to be superior to semaglutide, with estimated treatment differences of −0.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], −0.28 to −0.03; P=0.02) with tirzepatide 5 mg, −0.39 percentage points (95% CI, −0.51 to −0.26; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 10 mg, and −0.45 percentage points (95% CI, −0.57 to −0.32; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 15 mg. The glycated hemoglobin level over time is shown in (Figure 1B). A total of 82 to 86% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 79% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 7.0%. A total of 69 to 80% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 64% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to 6.5% or less, and 27 to 46% and 19%, respectively, had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 5.7% (Fig. 1C). A subgroup analysis involving patients with a glycated hemoglobin level above 8.5% showed mean reductions in the glycated hemoglobin level of −3.22 percentage points with tirzepatide at a dose of 15 mg as compared with −2.68 percentage points with semaglutide (Fig. S8). Figure 1 With the use of the efficacy estimand at 40 weeks, greater reductions in the fasting serum glucose level were observed with all tirzepatide doses than with semaglutide (Figure 1D and Fig. S9). .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:1:Title: Efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis URL Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full Published Time: 2022-10-28 Markdown Content: ## Abstract **Purpose:** A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to combine the data available from clinical trials and evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in people with type 2 diabetes (T2D). **Methods:** We systematically searched the MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) up to 25 March 2022 for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared tirzepatide with placebo or active hypoglycemic drugs in subjects with T2D. Heterogeneity was judged by the _I_ 2 value and Cochran’s Q test. The randomized effects model was adopted to calculate risk ratios and weighted mean differences (WMDs). The primary outcome was the change from baseline in HbA1c levels. Secondary efficacy endpoints were fasting serum glucose (FSG), change of body weight, blood pressure, fasting lipid profiles, and safety indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: −1.44, −0.56), FSG (WMD, −21.50
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:5:ty indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: −1.44, −0.56), FSG (WMD, −21.50 mg/dl; 95% CI: −34.44, −8.56), body weight (WMD: −7.99 kg; 95% CI −11.36, −4.62), and blood pressure and ameliorated fasting lipid profiles, without increasing hypoglycemia, either as monotherapy or an add-on therapy. .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:17: sequence, and conjugated with a fatty acid chain similar to the semaglutide side chain to promote its binding with albumin, so as to prolong the half-life of the drug ([Coskun et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B4)). A series of clinical trials on tirzepatide have explored its efficacy and safety in the treatment of T2D and shown great potential in decreasing hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) and body weight ([Min and Bain, 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B27)). There are two systematic reviews and meta-analyses on the evaluation of the effectiveness of tirzepatide on the treatment of diabetes ([Bhagavathula et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B2); [Dutta et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B10)). They both included one randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial which appraised the efficacy and tolerability of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes lasting for 12 weeks ([Frias et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B12)). However, the dose regimens in the study were dose-escalation. The duration of each dose was 2–4 weeks, and the treatment time of tirzepatide at the target stable dose was only 4 weeks, which was too short to judge the efficacy of a speci
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:21:sponse analysis. Finally, the results of SURPASS-5 were published in 2022 and were not included. We performed and updated a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the safety and efficacy of tirzepatide in patients with T2D. ## 2 Methods ### 2.1 Protocol Our systematic review and meta-analysis was executed and reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement ([Page et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B28)). The protocol was registered in PROSPERO (No. CRD42022319442). ### 2.2 Search strategy The Embase, MEDLINE, and Cochrane databases and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) were comprehensively searched prior to 25 March 2022, without limitations on language, race, or co
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:25:n OR dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist) AND (type 2 diabetes OR diabetes) AND (randomized controlled trial). The search strategy was adjusted to meet the requirements of each database. ### 2.3 Inclusion and exclusion criteria We included randomized controlled trials (RCTs), which lasted at least 12 weeks, that assessed and compared the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide with other hypoglycemic agents or placebos in T2D patients (≥18 years
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:29:diagnosed according to the World Health Organization (1999) or American Diabetes Association (1997) criteria. Reviews, letters, case reports, nonhuman studies, editorials, commentaries, expert opinions, non-RCTs, and meta-analyses were excluded. ### 2.4 Outcome measures of efficacy and safety The change in HbA1c from the baseline was considered the primary outcome of efficacy. The secondary endpoints included proportions of patients with HbA1c 5% of usual body weight (American College of Cardiology/[American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B1)). Our meta-analysis results demonstrated that loss of body weight in the tirzepatide group was from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg, whether used as monotherapy (7.40 kg) or add-on therapy (8.11 kg). Tirzepatide therapy led to a greater target of the percentage of patients who reached the body weight reduction of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%, whether used as monotherapy or add-on therapy. More interestingly, weight loss was observed at all doses of tirzepatide within 4 weeks after the start of treatment, and this continued until week 40 or 52; no dose of tirzepatide reached a plateau ([Ludvik et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B23); [Rosenstock et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B30)). The effect of tirzepatide on weight loss is expected. The subpopulation of the SURPASS-3 study showed that tirzepatide led to a significant reduction in liver fat content, volume of visceral adipose tissue, and abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue volumes compared with insulin degludec ([Gastaldelli et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B14)). GLP-1 RAs not only show a good hypoglycemic effect but also demonstrate the efficacy of weight loss. A meta-analysis evaluated the weight reduction effects of GLP-1 RAs and exhibited that, compared to placebo, GLP-1 RAs led to significant body weight reduction ([Htike et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B19)). As for semaglutide administered in patients with T2D, compared with placebo, subcutaneous semaglutide led to body weight loss (WMD: -2.73 kg and -4.09 kg, for 0.5 mg and 1 mg, respectively). Oral administration showed similar effects ([Zhong et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B38)). For treatment with dulaglutide in patients with T2D, the mean weight loss was 0.73 kg in the 0.75 mg dulaglutide group and 1.27 kg in the 1.5 mg dulaglutide group ([Qie et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B29)). Our meta-analysis included semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg as the control and presented that tirzepatide reduced body weight greater than the two GLP-1 RAs and resulted in a higher proportion of body weight reaching ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%. .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_000.txt:1:Abstract This article reviews the efficacy and safety data of tirzepatide, a once-weekly, novel glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist approved in the United States, the European Union, and other regions for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. All doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators. The safety profile of tirzepatide was consistent with that of the GLP-1 receptor agonist class, with mild to moderate and transient gastrointestinal side effects being the most common adverse events. With clinically and statistically significant reductions in A1C and body weight without increased risk of hypoglycemia in various populations, tirzepatide has demonstrated potential as a first-in-class treatment option for many people with type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is a metabolic condition caused by increased insulin resistance and progressive β-cell failure in the pancreas, resulting in decreased insulin secretion and hyperglycemia. Chronic hyperglycemia can ultimately lead to both micro- and macrovascular complications, which can be prevented with appropriate glycemic control. Obesity is a major factor in the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes; therefore, treatment of type 2 diabetes should address hyperglycemia and comorbidities ([1](#B1),[2](#B2)). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are commonly used as first injectable therapy
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_000.txt:5:rther studies are needed to evaluate these potential mechanisms of action in humans. A phase 2 study assessed the efficacy and safety of four doses (1, 5, 10, and 15 mg) of tirzepatide versus placebo and an active comparator (once-weekly dulaglutide 1.5 mg) in patients with type 2 diabetes ([20](#B20)). The study demonstrated the superiority of tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg versus placebo and dulaglutide 1.5 mg in decreasing A1C at week 26. Likewise, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg were superior to placebo in body weight reduction, and tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were superior to dulaglutide 1.5 mg in body weight reduction. Similar to the tirzepatide phase 1 study ([18](#B18)), most of the tirzepatide adverse events (AEs) were GI-related, with the incidence higher than that for the GLP-1 receptor agonist class. Additionally, a placebo-contr
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_000.txt:9:fficacy and safety of three doses of tirzepatide (5, 10, and 15 mg) once weekly in participants with type 2 diabetes. Based on the findings of the phase 2 studies, the dose escalation scheme in the SURPASS trials was as follows: all doses started at 2.5 mg once weekly for 4 weeks; then each dose was escalated every 4 weeks in increments of 2.5 mg until the corresponding maintenance dose of tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) was attained and maintained for the remainder of the study ([Figure 1](#F1)). Tirzepatide was compared with placebo as monotherapy in SURPASS-1 ([NCT03954834](https://clini

.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:7:      "full_text": "Abstract This article reviews the efficacy and safety data of tirzepatide, a once-weekly, novel glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist approved in the United States, the European Union, and other regions for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. All doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators. The safety profile of tirzepatide was consistent with that of the GLP-1 receptor agonist class, with mild to moderate and transient gastrointestinal side effects being the most common adverse events. With clinically and statistically significant reductions in A1C and body weight without increased risk of hypoglycemia in various populations, tirzepatide has demonstrated potential as a first-in-class treatment option for many people with type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is a metabolic condition caused by increased insulin resistance and progressive \u03b2-cell failure in the pancreas, resulting in decreased insulin secretion and hyperglycemia. Chronic hyperglycemia can ultimately lead to both micro- and macrovascular complications, which can be prevented with appropriate glycemic control. Obesity is a major factor in the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes; therefore, treatment of type 2 diabetes should address hyperglycemia and comorbidities ([1](#B1),[2](#B2)). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are commonly used as first injectable therapy\n\n[...]\n\nrther studies are needed to evaluate these potential mechanisms of action in humans. A phase 2 study assessed the efficacy and safety of four doses (1, 5, 10, and 15 mg) of tirzepatide versus placebo and an active comparator (once-weekly dulaglutide 1.5 mg) in patients with type 2 diabetes ([20](#B20)). The study demonstrated the superiority of tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg versus placebo and dulaglutide 1.5 mg in decreasing A1C at week 26. Likewise, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg were superior to placebo in body weight reduction, and tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were superior to dulaglutide 1.5 mg in body weight reduction. Similar to the tirzepatide phase 1 study ([18](#B18)), most of the tirzepatide adverse events (AEs) were GI-related, with the incidence higher than that for the GLP-1 receptor agonist class. Additionally, a placebo-contr\n\n[...]\n\nfficacy and safety of three doses of tirzepatide (5, 10, and 15 mg) once weekly in participants with type 2 diabetes. Based on the findings of the phase 2 studies, the dose escalation scheme in the SURPASS trials was as follows: all doses started at 2.5 mg once weekly for 4 weeks; then each dose was escalated every 4 weeks in increments of 2.5 mg until the corresponding maintenance dose of tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) was attained and maintained for the remainder of the study ([Figure 1](#F1)). Tirzepatide was compared with placebo as monotherapy in SURPASS-1 ([NCT03954834](https://clini\n\n[...]\n\nblind trials, whereas SURPASS-2, SURPASS-3, and SURPASS-4 were open-label trials. The primary efficacy end point for these studies was the change from baseline in A1C. Glycemic values must be personalized based on patient preference, risk of hypoglycemia and other AEs of therapy, and patient comorbidities. The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0%, \u22646.5%, or <5.7% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%, from 66 to 95%, and from 23 to 62%, respectively ([Table 2](#T2)). Significantly more participants achieved the three A1C values with all tirzepatide doses in each of the f\n\n[...]\n\nm initiating therapy. Therefore, broadening the treatment continuum with therapies that achieve glycemic control with weight loss effects is important. In the SURPASS trials, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from \u22126.2 kg (\u22126.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to \u221212.9 kg (\u221213.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3) ([Table 2](#T2) and [Figure 2B](#F2)). In each of the SURPASS clinical trials, body weight reductions were significantly greater for all tirzepatide dose\n\n[...]\n\nS studies compared with placebo or active comparators at the primary end point. There was an overall improvement in the fasting lipid profile with tirzepatide across the SURPASS trials. Namely, there was a decrease from baseline in triglycerides (by 15.2\u221226.7%), LDL cholesterol (by 5.2\u221215.5%), and VLDL cholesterol (by 14.2\u221225.2%) and an increase from baseline in HDL cholesterol (by 0.9\u221210.8%) with all tirzepatide doses at the primary end point. Decreases in systolic (of 2.8\u221212.6 mmHg) and diastolic (of 0.8\u22124.5 mmHg) blood pressure were also observed with tirzepatide in all SURPASS trials. .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:14:      "snippet": "Abstract This article reviews the efficacy and safety data of tirzepatide, a once-weekly, novel glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist approv",
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:23:      "full_text": "Title: Efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis URL Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full Published Time: 2022-10-28 Markdown Content: ## Abstract **Purpose:** A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to combine the data available from clinical trials and evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in people with type 2 diabetes (T2D). **Methods:** We systematically searched the MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) up to 25 March 2022 for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared tirzepatide with placebo or active hypoglycemic drugs in subjects with T2D. Heterogeneity was judged by the _I_ 2 value and Cochran\u2019s Q test. The randomized effects model was adopted to calculate risk ratios and weighted mean differences (WMDs). The primary outcome was the change from baseline in HbA1c levels. Secondary efficacy endpoints were fasting serum glucose (FSG), change of body weight, blood pressure, fasting lipid profiles, and safety indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: \u22121.44, \u22120.56), FSG (WMD, \u221221.50\n\n[...]\n\nty indexes. **Results:** Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: \u22121.44, \u22120.56), FSG (WMD, \u221221.50 mg/dl; 95% CI: \u221234.44, \u22128.56), body weight (WMD: \u22127.99 kg; 95% CI \u221211.36, \u22124.62), and blood pressure and ameliorated fasting lipid profiles, without increasing hypoglycemia, either as monotherapy or an add-on therapy. Tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events mainly in add-on therapy but not in terms of\n\n[...]\n\netes has become one of the most prevalent non-communicable chronic diseases, resulting in disabilities, expensive complications, and even shortening of life expectancy ([Saeedi et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B32)). Unfortunately, in the world, the prevalence rate of diabetes in the population of age 20\u201379 years was 10.5% in 2021, which was about 536 million 600 thousand; by 2045, it is expected to increase up to 12.2%, which will be about 783 million 200 thousand ([Sun H. et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B37)). According to the pathophysiology of diabetes, different antihyperglycemic drugs have been developed for clinical application. Since human insulin was approved in 1982, the FDA has approved 59 drugs for controlling hyperglycemia ([Dahlen et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B6)). Among these antihyperglycemic agents, incretin-dependent T2D therapies play an important role. As a 30-amino-acid-peptide, glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) exerts biological effects as an incretin-stimulating hormone. The first batch of in\n\n[...]\n\ne used increasingly and show excellent clinical benefits including decreased glucose and body weight, lowered cardiovascular risk, and reduced risk of hypoglycemia ([Lyseng-Williamson, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B24)). Glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP), the other incretin hormone, is similar to GLP-1 and its receptor; however, GIP does not inhibit appetite and food intake ([Holst and Rosenkilde, 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B18)). Thus, it is assumed that the combination of GLP-1 RA with other drugs acting on GIP receptors may produce more effective blood glucose control and weight loss. Tirzepatide, a dual GIP and GLP-1 RA, is a polypeptide containing 39 amino ac\n\n[...]\n\n sequence, and conjugated with a fatty acid chain similar to the semaglutide side chain to promote its binding with albumin, so as to prolong the half-life of the drug ([Coskun et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B4)). A series of clinical trials on tirzepatide have explored its efficacy and safety in the treatment of T2D and shown great potential in decreasing hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) and body weight ([Min and Bain, 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B27)). There are two systematic reviews and meta-analyses on the evaluation of the effectiveness of tirzepatide on the treatment of diabetes ([Bhagavathula et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B2); [Dutta et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B10)). They both included one randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial which appraised the efficacy and tolerability of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes lasting for 12 weeks ([Frias et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B12)). However, the dose regimens in the study were dose-escalation. The duration of each dose was 2\u20134 weeks, and the treatment time of tirzepatide at the target stable dose was only 4 weeks, which was too short to judge the efficacy of a speci\n\n[...]\n\nsponse analysis. Finally, the results of SURPASS-5 were published in 2022 and were not included. We performed and updated a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the safety and efficacy of tirzepatide in patients with T2D. ## 2 Methods ### 2.1 Protocol Our systematic review and meta-analysis was executed and reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement ([Page et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B28)). The protocol was registered in PROSPERO (No. CRD42022319442). ### 2.2 Search strategy The Embase, MEDLINE, and Cochrane databases and clinical trials registries ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)) were comprehensively searched prior to 25 March 2022, without limitations on language, race, or co\n\n[...]\n\nn OR dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist) AND (type 2 diabetes OR diabetes) AND (randomized controlled trial). The search strategy was adjusted to meet the requirements of each database. ### 2.3 Inclusion and exclusion criteria We included randomized controlled trials (RCTs), which lasted at least 12 weeks, that assessed and compared the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide with other hypoglycemic agents or placebos in T2D patients (\u226518 years\n\n[...]\n\ndiagnosed according to the World Health Organization (1999) or American Diabetes Association (1997) criteria. Reviews, letters, case reports, nonhuman studies, editorials, commentaries, expert opinions, non-RCTs, and meta-analyses were excluded. ### 2.4 Outcome measures of efficacy and safety The change in HbA1c from the baseline was considered the primary outcome of efficacy. The secondary endpoints included proportions of patients with HbA1c 5% of usual body weight (American College of Cardiology/[American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B1)). Our meta-analysis results demonstrated that loss of body weight in the tirzepatide group was from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg, whether used as monotherapy (7.40 kg) or add-on therapy (8.11 kg). Tirzepatide therapy led to a greater target of the percentage of patients who reached the body weight reduction of \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515%, whether used as monotherapy or add-on therapy. More interestingly, weight loss was observed at all doses of tirzepatide within 4 weeks after the start of treatment, and this continued until week 40 or 52; no dose of tirzepatide reached a plateau ([Ludvik et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B23); [Rosenstock et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B30)). The effect of tirzepatide on weight loss is expected. The subpopulation of the SURPASS-3 study showed that tirzepatide led to a significant reduction in liver fat content, volume of visceral adipose tissue, and abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue volumes compared with insulin degludec ([Gastaldelli et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B14)). GLP-1 RAs not only show a good hypoglycemic effect but also demonstrate the efficacy of weight loss. A meta-analysis evaluated the weight reduction effects of GLP-1 RAs and exhibited that, compared to placebo, GLP-1 RAs led to significant body weight reduction ([Htike et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B19)). As for semaglutide administered in patients with T2D, compared with placebo, subcutaneous semaglutide led to body weight loss (WMD: -2.73 kg and -4.09 kg, for 0.5 mg and 1 mg, respectively). Oral administration showed similar effects ([Zhong et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B38)). For treatment with dulaglutide in patients with T2D, the mean weight loss was 0.73 kg in the 0.75 mg dulaglutide group and 1.27 kg in the 1.5 mg dulaglutide group ([Qie et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B29)). Our meta-analysis included semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg as the control and presented that tirzepatide reduced body weight greater than the two GLP-1 RAs and resulted in a higher proportion of body weight reaching \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515%. Blood pressure and lipid profiles are the risk factors of cardiovasc",

Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes Published June 25, 2021 N Engl J Med 2021;385:503-515 DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2107519 Abstract Background Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that is under development for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. The efficacy and safety of once-weekly tirzepatide as compared with semaglutide, a selective GLP-1 receptor agonist, are unknown. Methods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-\n\n[...]\n\nMethods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-mg tirzepatide groups and the semaglutide group were \u22120.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], \u22120.28 to \u22120.03; P=0.02), \u22120.39 percentage points (95% CI, \u22120.51 to \u22120.26; P 8.5% [\u226469 or >69 mmol per mol]). Tirzepatide was initiated at a dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, and the doses were increased by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks until the randomly assigned dose was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial. Semaglutide was initiated at a dose of 0.25 mg once weekly, and the dose was doubled every 4 weeks until 1 mg was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial.20 Dose de-escalation was not allowed. The initiation of new antihyperglycemic medications was allowed ac\n\n[...]\n\neme, described in the Statistical Analyses Methods section in the Supplementary Appendix, Figs. S2 through S6, and Table S2) were the change in body weight from baseline to week 40 and the attainment of glycated hemoglobin level targets of less than 7.0% and less than 5.7%. Other end points were attainment of a glycated hemoglobin level of 6.5% or less and weight loss of at least 5%, 10%, or 15%; the mean change from baseline in the fasting serum glucose level and in the daily, patient-measured, mean seven-point blood glucose profiles (i.e., the mean of seven measurements); BMI and waist circumference; lipid levels; the results of an updated homeostasis model assessment\u2013insulin resistance (HOMA2-IR); and the fasting glucagon level adjusted for the fasting serum glucose level. Tirzepatide is an investigational once-weekly, dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist that integrates the actions of both incretins into a single molecule. The multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel, placebo-controlled SURMOUNT-3 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04657016](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04657016?term=NCT04657016&rank=1)) evaluated the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for 72 weeks after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention lead-in period in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities, excluding type 2 diabetes. At the end of the 12-week period, study participants (N=579) achieved 6.9% mean weight loss and were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive tirzepatide or placebo subcutaneously once weekly. The efficacy estimand (efficacy prior to discontinuation of study drug) results demonstrated that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 21.1% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 3.3% weight regain for the placebo group. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. codex
There is a concrete numeric-boundary issue: the helper searches required numbers with plain `str.find` and later `d in window_text`, while the strict span path compares regex-extracted numeric tokens. That means the fallback can match `1.07` inside `-1.07`, or `50` inside `150`.
exec
"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command '$env:PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT='"'off'; @'
from src.polaris_graph.generator.provenance_generator import verify_sentence_provenance, _find_local_support_window

print('positive inside negative window:', _find_local_support_window({'1.07'}, {'tirzepatide','patients'}, 'tirzepatide patients had -1.07 percentage-point change in HbA1c'))
print('integer inside larger integer window:', _find_local_support_window({'50'}, {'patients','type'}, 'type 2 diabetes trial enrolled 150 patients'))

ev = {'ev1': {'direct_quote': 'tirzepatide patients with type 2 diabetes had a -1.07 percentage-point change in HbA1c.'}}
s = 'tirzepatide patients with type 2 diabetes had a 1.07 percentage-point change in HbA1c [#ev:ev1:0-45].'
r = verify_sentence_provenance(s, ev)
print('verified:', r.is_verified)
print('failures:', r.failure_reasons)

    1: """I-bug-098 — entailment judge wired into PRODUCTION verifier.
    2: 
    3: Mirrors tests/polaris_graph/clinical_generator/test_strict_verify_entailment.py

    5: provenance_generator.py:verify_sentence_provenance) directly. This is

    7: actually uses; the I-bug-092..097 clinical_generator/ tests cover the slice-003
    8: demo path which is a different code branch.
    9: 
   10: Tests use a fake judge to keep CI network-free + deterministic. Live
   11: canary lives at tests/polaris_graph/clinical_generator/
   12: test_strict_verify_entailment_live.py (env-gated).
   13: """
   14: 
   15: from __future__ import annotations
   16: 
   17: import pytest
   18: 
   19: from polaris_graph.clinical_generator import strict_verify as _gen2
   20: from src.polaris_graph.generator.provenance_generator import (
   21:     verify_sentence_provenance,
   22: )
   23: 
   24: 
   25: # ---------- Fake-judge fixture (shared with clinical_generator telemetry) ----------
   26: 
   27: class _FakeJudge:
   28:     def __init__(self, verdict: str, reason: str = "fake") -> None:
   29:         self.verdict = verdict
   30:         self.reason = reason
   31:         self.calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
   32: 
   33:     def judge(self, sentence: str, span: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
   34:         self.calls.append((sentence, span))
   35:         return self.verdict, self.reason
   36: 
   37: 
   38: def _install(monkeypatch, fake: _FakeJudge) -> None:
   39:     """Replace both the judge singleton and the _get_judge factory so
   40:     verify_sentence_provenance picks up our fake.
   41:     """
   42:     monkeypatch.setattr(_gen2, "_JUDGE_SINGLETON", fake, raising=False)
   43:     monkeypatch.setattr(_gen2, "_get_judge", lambda: fake)
   44: 
   45: 
   46: @pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
   47: def _reset_telemetry():
   48:     """Ensure each test starts with zero counters."""
   49:     _gen2.reset_judge_telemetry()
   50: 
   51: 
   52: # ---------- Helpers — build evidence pool dicts the production way ----------
   53: 
   54: def _pool(direct_quote: str, evidence_id: str = "ev_x") -> dict:
   55:     return {
   56:         evidence_id: {

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:585:outputs/full_scale_v7/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm\report.md:17:Meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials demonstrate a dose-dependent relationship for tirzepatide's effects on HbA1c and body weight in type 2 diabetes.[17] Another meta-analysis confirmed these trends, finding HbA1c reductions of 0.20% with 10 mg and 0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and weight reductions of 2.64 kg and 4.26 kg, respectively.[18] A more recent network meta-analysis also found significant reductions in HbA1c with tirzepatide 10 mg (MD: -0.19) and 15 mg (MD: -0.32) versus the 5 mg dose, alongside weight reductions of 1.96 kg and 3.31 kg, respectively.[19] Regarding the time to achieve clinical thresholds, a pre-planned exploratory analysis of the SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 trials found the median time to first achieve a body weight loss of ≥5% was faster with tirzepatide 5 mg (16.0 weeks) and with the 10 and 15 mg doses (12.4 weeks) than with the active comparator semaglutide 1 mg (24.0 weeks).[20] The same analysis reported the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for each tirzepatide dose.[20] In terms of safety, meta-analyses indicate the risk of total adverse events and gastrointestinal adverse events is higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[17][18] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy, but the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[17]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:225:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:1:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:226:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:3:### Efficacy
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:227:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:5:In the SURPASS-1 trial of patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone, tirzepatide at 5, 10, and 15 mg doses reduced HbA1c by −1.87%, −1.89%, and −2.07% from baseline versus a 0.04% increase with placebo at 40 weeks.[1][2] Tirzepatide also demonstrated superior efficacy versus basal insulins; in SURPASS-3, it reduced HbA1c by −1.93% to −2.37% across doses versus −1.34% with insulin degludec over 52 weeks, and in SURPASS-4, it reduced HbA1c by −2.24% to −2.58% versus −1.44% with insulin glargine.[1][3][4] For patients on basal insulin with inadequate control, adding tirzepatide in SURPASS-6 was more effective than adding prandial insulin lispro, reducing HbA1c by −2.1% versus −1.1% and reducing body weight by −6.7 to −11.0 kg versus a 3.2 kg gain.[1][5] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets, with 57% of patients on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7.0%, weight loss >5%, systolic blood pressure <130 mmHg) versus 34% on semaglutide.[6] Efficacy was consistent across key subgroups; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, greater HbA1c reductions were seen in patients with higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%), and greater weight loss was seen in those with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[7] A pre-planned analysis showed tirzepatide enabled faster achievement of glycemic and weight loss thresholds than semaglutide 1 mg or insulin degludec, with a median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 and 12.1 weeks, respectively.[8]
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:228:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:7:### Safety
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:229:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:9:Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide across phase 3 trials in type 2 diabetes.[1][9] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in an Asia-Pacific population, gastrointestinal adverse event incidence was generally similar across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, diabetes duration, and concomitant oral medications.[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of nine RCTs concluded the gastrointestinal adverse event profile of tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1 receptor agonists, with incidences of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea higher than placebo but similar to active comparators like semaglutide and dulaglutide.[10] A systematic review and meta-analysis concluded tirzepatide did not increase events of blood glucose <54 mg/dL or <70 mg/dL compared to control overall.[11] However, when tirzepatide is added to insulin or sulfonylureas, the risk increases; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, where patients were on metformin with or without a sulfonylurea, the incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose ≤70 mg/dL or severe) was higher in patients using a concomitant sulfonylurea (e.g., 43.1% with tirzepatide 5 mg) compared to those on metformin alone (6.6% with tirzepatide 5 mg).[7] One meta-analysis specifically noted tirzepatide 15 mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83), while another network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide.[10][12] In clinical trials, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) were reported; for instance, the SURMOUNT-4 trial specifically stated no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma or pancreatic cancer were reported.[13] However, when compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists, tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer.[14] A 2026 evidence review summarizing multiple large database studies concluded there is no evidence of increased thyroid cancer risk with GLP-1 receptor agonist use, including tirzepatide, over 1.8-3.0 years of follow-up.[15] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal cardiovascular and obesity outcome trials, including the SURPASS program, did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[16]
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:230:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:11:### Comparative
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:231:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:13:A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide found that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, with tirzepatide 15 mg ranked as the most efficacious treatment.[17] Tirzepatide also enabled more patients to achieve stringent glycemic targets faster than semaglutide 1 mg, with a median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 weeks and to achieve HbA1c ≤6.5% of 12.1 weeks versus 15.7 weeks.[8] A meta-analysis of direct comparative studies in overweight and obese adults, which included populations with and without type 2 diabetes, found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide for weight reduction, with a standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI 0.52 to 0.92) and greater odds of achieving ≥10% weight loss (OR 0.21, 95% CI 0.06 to 0.78).[18] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 reported that tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving an intensive weight loss target of >15% compared to semaglutide (OR 3.86, 95% CI 2.69 to 5.55).[6] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and significant weight loss, while insulin degludec was associated with weight gain.[3] Furthermore, a network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the GLP-1 RAs dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <54 mg/dL) was low and similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 1 mg.[20] The network meta-analysis comparing subcutaneous agents found both tirzepatide and semaglutide increased the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to placebo, with no increased risk of serious adverse events or severe hypoglycemia for either.[17]
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:232:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:15:### Dose Response
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:233:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:17:Tirzepatide demonstrates a clear dose-dependent effect on both glycemic control and weight reduction in patients with type 2 diabetes.[1][21][22] Another meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with tirzepatide 15 mg achieving the best efficacy.[21] The time to achieve glycemic and weight loss targets is also dose-responsive and faster with higher tirzepatide doses compared to active comparators.[8] A pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to first achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all tirzepatide doses, compared to 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec.[8] Furthermore, the median time to achieve ≥5% weight loss in SURPASS-2 was 16.0 weeks with tirzepatide 5 mg and 12.4 weeks with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses, compared to 24.0 weeks with semaglutide 1 mg.[8] Subgroup analyses indicate that the efficacy of tirzepatide is generally consistent across different patient demographics, though some variations exist.[7] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, patients with a higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%) experienced significantly greater HbA1c reductions with all tirzepatide doses, while patients with a higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater weight reductions across all doses.[7] An indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater HbA1c and body weight reductions compared to semaglutide 2.4 mg (an obesity dose in a related population), while tirzepatide 5 mg showed similar efficacy to semaglutide 2.4 mg.[23]
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:234:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:19:### Regulatory
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:235:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the trade name Mounjaro in 2022 as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24][25] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The FDA label carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, based on findings in rats, and contraindicates tirzepatide in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2.[26] The FDA later approved tirzepatide for chronic weight management under the trade name Zepbound in 2023, indicated as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for adults with obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥27 kg/m²) with at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27][28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for Mounjaro valid throughout the EU on 15 September 2022.[29] The EMA's approved indication for type 2 diabetes is for the treatment of adults, adolescents, and children aged 10 years and above with insufficiently controlled disease as an adjunct to diet and exercise, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic products.[30] The EMA also approved an indication for weight management, defined as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31][30][32] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific problems associated with obesity, or a BMI below 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE's recommendation is contingent upon a commercial arrangement that makes tirzepatide available to the NHS at a cost-effective price.[33][34] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide as an option for people with a BMI of more than 35 and at least one weight-related illness, with an initial rollout prioritized for those in specialist services.[35] Key labeling warnings and precautions are consistent across authorities and include risks of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, hypersensitivity reactions, and hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin or insulin secretagogues.[36][26][30] The FDA and EMA labels also warn that tirzepatide delays gastric emptying, which can impact the absorption of concomitantly administered oral medications, necessitating guidance for patients using oral contraceptives.[25][37][30] The Canadian Product Monograph for Zepbound similarly contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma and warns of gastrointestinal adverse reactions and the potential for drug interactions due to delayed gastric emptying.[38]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:3566:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the trade name Mounjaro in 2022 as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24][25] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The FDA label carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, based on findings in rats, and contraindicates tirzepatide in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2.[26] The FDA later approved tirzepatide for chronic weight management under the trade name Zepbound in 2023, indicated as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for adults with obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥27 kg/m²) with at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27][28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for Mounjaro valid throughout the EU on 15 September 2022.[29] The EMA's approved indication for type 2 diabetes is for the treatment of adults, adolescents, and children aged 10 years and above with insufficiently controlled disease as an adjunct to diet and exercise, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic products.[30] The EMA also approved an indication for weight management, defined as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31][30][32] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific problems associated with obesity, or a BMI below 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE's recommendation is contingent upon a commercial arrangement that makes tirzepatide available to the NHS at a cost-effective price.[33][34] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide as an option for people with a BMI of more than 35 and at least one weight-related illness, with an initial rollout prioritized for those in specialist services.[35] Key labeling warnings and precautions are consistent across authorities and include risks of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, hypersensitivity reactions, and hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin or insulin secretagogues.[36][26][30] The FDA and EMA labels also warn that tirzepatide delays gastric emptying, which can impact the absorption of concomitantly administered oral medications, necessitating guidance for patients using oral contraceptives.[25][37][30] The Canadian Product Monograph for Zepbound similarly contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma and warns of gastrointestinal adverse reactions and the potential for drug interactions due to delayed gastric emptying.[38]

outputs\codex_findings\m9_review\findings.md:8:   The phase matcher is not aligned to the real V30 sweep output. The checked-in phase-2 runs, including run-14, emit `[scope]`, `[retrieval]`, `[adequacy]`, `[generation]`, `[judge]`, `[eval_gate]`, `[V30-P2]`, `[V30]`, and `[status]`, not the canonical strings used here (`scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:466-468,592,666-681,1052-1053,1094-1100,1201-1202,1693-1714,1750,1896-1923`; `outputs/full_scale_v30_phase2_run14/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/run_log.txt:6,9,12,17-20,27,33-40,42`). Running the current classifier over run-14 only yields `generation_started` and `v30_phase1`, so a live job will effectively jump `1 -> 70 -> 90 -> completed`. Also, the current milestone order is not the script’s real order: `[V30-P2]` logs happen before evaluator/judge, while the `M-56` / `[V30]` lines happen after `eval_gate`. If you only add missing patterns without reordering milestones, progress will regress.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_deep_content_audit\findings.md:7:**V28 says.** "In the head-to-head SURPASS-2 trial (N=1879, baseline HbA1c 7.0-10.5%), once-weekly tirzepatide at 5, 10, and 15 mg demonstrated superior reductions in HbA1c and body weight at 40 weeks compared to once-weekly semaglutide 1 mg.[20]" (outputs/full_scale_v28/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:13). V28 also says SURPASS-2 randomized N=1879 on metformin to tirzepatide 5/10/15 mg vs semaglutide 1 mg for 40 weeks (report.md:17), but [20] is "a post hoc analysis of the SURPASS-2 Trial" and tier T4 (report.md:112), not the Frías NEJM primary publication.
outputs\codex_findings\v27_deep_content_audit\findings.md:5:**V27 claim.** "A post hoc analysis of this trial found that all tirzepatide doses increased the proportion of patients achieving composite therapeutic targets ... compared to semaglutide 1 mg, with 57% ... versus 34% on semaglutide.[15]" (outputs/full_scale_v27/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:13). It also cites an aITC versus semaglutide 0.5 mg at week 40 rather than the primary head-to-head trial (line 13; bibliography [15]-[16] at report.md:78-79). The primary NEJM SURPASS-2 source is present in V27's live corpus (live_corpus_dump.json:44-45, 1202-1203) but not selected in the final bibliography.

## What Step 1 was supposed to fix (your prior P1 #1)

Per your last verdict
(`.codex/I-gen-005/codex_verifier_bug_verdict_iter1.txt`):

> "P1: Full-direct_quote fallback is too permissive. provenance_generator.py
> lines 719-753 accepts a sentence if all missing decimals appear
> anywhere in the cited evidence's full direct_quote. That can validate
> a claim assembled from unrelated parts of a 25k-char review/page.
> Your cancer 50% example is a real failure mode if the narrow span
> shares two content words and the full document contains '50%'
> elsewhere."

Your refinement proposal:

> "Middle ground: allow a trial name from direct_quote only when it
> appears in the cited span or in a repaired local support window
> that also contains the sentence's claim numbers/comparator terms."

This Step 1 implements the local-support-window concept for NUMBERS
(decimals + integers). Trial-name alias work is deferred to Step 3.

## What I implemented (the diff)

The full diff is at `/tmp/step1_diff.patch` (322 lines, +263 / -55).
Live source: `src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py`.

### Change 1 — new helper `_find_local_support_window()`

```python
def _find_local_support_window(
    needed_decimals: set[str],
    needed_content_words: set[str],
    direct_quote: str,
    window: int = 400,
    min_content_overlap: int = 2,
) -> Optional[tuple[int, int]]:
    """Find a contiguous window in direct_quote that contains ALL the
    sentence's missing decimals AND at least min_content_overlap content
    words from the sentence."""
    if not needed_decimals or not direct_quote:
        return None
    norm = _normalize_unicode_minus(direct_quote)
    n = len(norm)

    # Find all occurrences of each needed decimal
    positions_per_decimal: dict[str, list[int]] = {}
    for d in needed_decimals:
        positions = []
        i = 0
        while True:
            idx = norm.find(d, i)
            if idx < 0: break
            positions.append(idx)
            i = idx + 1
        if not positions:
            return None  # decimal absent from doc → truly missing
        positions_per_decimal[d] = positions

    # Anchor on rarest decimal
    rarest = min(positions_per_decimal, key=lambda d: len(positions_per_decimal[d]))

    # Try window placements at each anchor
    for anchor in positions_per_decimal[rarest]:
        for ws_offset in (0, -window // 2, -window + len(rarest)):
            window_start = max(0, anchor + ws_offset)
            window_end = min(n, window_start + window)
            window_text = norm[window_start:window_end]
            window_lower = window_text.lower()

            if not all(d in window_text for d in needed_decimals):
                continue

            if needed_content_words:
                overlap = sum(1 for w in needed_content_words if w in window_lower)
                if overlap < min_content_overlap:
                    continue

            return (window_start, window_end)

    return None
```

### Change 2 — replace whole-doc decimal fallback with local-window

```python
if missing_in_span:
    # I-gen-005 Step 1 (Codex P1 #1): replace prior whole-document
    # fallback with safe LOCAL-WINDOW check.
    found_window: Optional[tuple[int, int]] = None
    found_ev_id: Optional[str] = None
    for tok in tokens:
        ev = evidence_pool.get(tok.evidence_id)
        if ev is None:
            continue
        direct_quote = ev.get("direct_quote") or ev.get("statement") or ""
        win = _find_local_support_window(
            missing_in_span,
            sentence_content_for_window,  # _content_words(sentence_stripped)
            direct_quote,
            window=400,
            min_content_overlap=2,
        )
        if win:
            found_window = win
            found_ev_id = tok.evidence_id
            break

    ev_ids = ",".join(sorted({t.evidence_id for t in tokens}))
    if found_window:
        logger.warning("[provenance] local_support_window_found ev=%s "
                       "window=%d-%d missing=%s — span_imprecise but "
                       "locally grounded; passing",
                       found_ev_id, found_window[0], found_window[1],
                       sorted(missing_in_span))
    else:
        failures.append(
            f"number_not_in_any_cited_span:{ev_ids}:"
            f"missing={sorted(missing_in_span)}"
        )
```

```
Sentence: "In a systematic review and meta-analysis of six randomized
```

```
[#ev:ev_001:0-500]."

Result: verified=False

           "no_content_word_overlap_any_cited_span:ev_001:
            sentence_words=['cancer', 'cases', 'cures', 'elderly',

```

Both behave as your P1 #1 demanded.

## Questions for you, Codex (find holes)

1. **Window size of 400** — is that defensible? Too narrow: misses
   table rows that span >400 bytes. Too wide: cancer-50% might find
   "50%" + 2 incidental content words like "patients" + "type" within
   400 bytes. Justify or push back.

2. **min_content_overlap=2** — is 2 content words enough to claim
   semantic alignment? Could a 400-char paragraph contain "patients"
   + "type" + a coincidental "50%" and pass? Specifically check the
   tirzepatide evidence pool for false-positive risk.

3. **Three window placements** (anchor at start / middle / end). Is
   that sufficient? Could the right window NEVER align with any of
   the three anchor positions if the needed decimals are spread
   across the document?

4. **Anchor on rarest decimal** — is this the right minimization
   strategy? Consider: what if the rarest decimal appears 50 times
   and the other 4 needed decimals appear 1000+ times in unrelated
   passages? We anchor on rarest but the window is still random for
   the others.

5. **`_normalize_unicode_minus` is called inside `_find_local_support_window`
   on `direct_quote`** but the `needed_decimals` set has ALREADY been
   normalized upstream by `_decimals_in`. Is there a double-normalize
   risk where `−1.07` becomes `--1.07` somewhere? Walk the code.

6. **Range-dash issue you flagged**: my current code maps `−` (U+2212)
   to `-`. So "8.12-8.21" stays as ASCII range. But what about "8.12—8.21"
   (em-dash)? After `_normalize_unicode_minus` it becomes "8.12-8.21".
   Then `_DECIMAL_NUMBER_RE = r"-?\d+\.\d+"` would extract "8.12" and
   "-8.21". The negative is fake. Step 1 does NOT fix this — it's
   deferred to Step 4. Is that defensible deferral or does it
   invalidate Step 1 alone?

7. **Bigger architectural question** — my Step 1 still patches verifier
   acceptance (just more tightly). Your P1 #2 was: "Many bad spans
   are produced by the span rewriter after the model emits [ev_XXX].
   Safer fix: recover one or more local support windows and verify
   those, not whole-document pass-through." Step 1 verifies local
   support windows — does that satisfy your P1 #2 spirit, or do I
   still need to fix `live_deepseek_generator._find_best_span_for_sentence`
   in Step 2?

8. **Predict the smoke result.** Given Step 1, what should the new
   drop counts look like vs the prior smoke (number_not_in: 5,
   trial_name: 13, entailment: 15, content_word: 1, integer: 2)?
   I'll compare your prediction to actual smoke and call out
   surprises.

+def _normalize_unicode_minus(text: str) -> str:
+    """I-gen-005 fix: replace unicode minus (U+2212), en-dash (U+2013),
+    em-dash (U+2014), and figure-dash (U+2012) with ASCII '-' so number
+    extraction matches consistently. Evidence text scraped from PDFs /
+    HTML often uses unicode minus (`−1.07`) while LLM output uses ASCII
+    (`-1.07`); raw set comparison previously failed for grounded
+    sentences because `{'-1.07'} != {'−1.07'}` byte-wise. This
+    normalization closes that mismatch."""
+    if not text:
+        return text
+    return (
+        text.replace("−", "-")  # U+2212 MINUS SIGN
+            .replace("–", "-")  # U+2013 EN DASH
+            .replace("—", "-")  # U+2014 EM DASH
+            .replace("‒", "-")  # U+2012 FIGURE DASH
+    )
+
+
 def _numbers_in(text: str) -> set[str]:
-    return {m.group(0) for m in _NUMBER_RE.finditer(text or "")}
+    text = _normalize_unicode_minus(text or "")
+    return {m.group(0) for m in _NUMBER_RE.finditer(text)}
 
 
 def _decimals_in(text: str) -> set[str]:
-    return {m.group(0) for m in _DECIMAL_NUMBER_RE.finditer(text or "")}
+    text = _normalize_unicode_minus(text or "")
+    return {m.group(0) for m in _DECIMAL_NUMBER_RE.finditer(text)}
+
+
+def _find_local_support_window(
+    needed_decimals: set[str],
+    needed_content_words: set[str],
+    direct_quote: str,
+    window: int = 400,
+    min_content_overlap: int = 2,
+) -> Optional[tuple[int, int]]:
+    """I-gen-005 Step 1 (Codex P1 #1 safety fix): find a contiguous
+    window in `direct_quote` that contains ALL the sentence's missing
+    decimals AND at least `min_content_overlap` content words from the
+    sentence.
+
+    Replaces the prior whole-document fallback (Codex flagged as too
+    permissive — could validate "cancer 50%" pulled from unrelated
+    paragraphs if 50% appears anywhere in the cited document).
+
+    Returns (window_start, window_end) byte offsets in `direct_quote`
+    where the local support was found, or None if no single window
+    contains both the required numbers and minimum content overlap.
+
+    Algorithm:
+      1. Find all byte offsets of each required decimal in normalized
+         text.
+      2. If any decimal is absent from the document, return None
+         (truly missing).
+      3. Anchor on the RAREST decimal (fewest occurrences = smallest
+         search space).
+      4. For each anchor position, try 3 window placements (anchor at
+         start / middle / end of window) and check:
+           - all required decimals occur within `window` bytes
+           - >= `min_content_overlap` content words also in window
+      5. Return first window that satisfies both. None otherwise.
+    """
+    if not needed_decimals or not direct_quote:
+        return None
+
+    norm = _normalize_unicode_minus(direct_quote)
+    norm_lower = norm.lower()
+    n = len(norm)
+
+    # 1. Find all occurrences of each needed decimal.
+    positions_per_decimal: dict[str, list[int]] = {}
+    for d in needed_decimals:
+        positions: list[int] = []
+        i = 0
+        while True:
+            idx = norm.find(d, i)
+            if idx < 0:
+                break
+            positions.append(idx)
+            i = idx + 1
+        if not positions:
+            # 2. Decimal not in document at all — truly missing.
+            return None
+        positions_per_decimal[d] = positions
+
+    # 3. Anchor on rarest decimal.
+    rarest = min(positions_per_decimal, key=lambda d: len(positions_per_decimal[d]))
+
+    # 4. For each anchor, try window placements.
+    for anchor in positions_per_decimal[rarest]:
+        for ws_offset in (0, -window // 2, -window + len(rarest)):
+            window_start = max(0, anchor + ws_offset)
+            window_end = min(n, window_start + window)
+            window_text = norm[window_start:window_end]
+            window_lower = window_text.lower()
+
+            # All decimals in window?
+            if not all(d in window_text for d in needed_decimals):
+                continue
+
+            # >= min_content_overlap content words in window?
+            if needed_content_words:
+                overlap = sum(1 for w in needed_content_words if w in window_lower)
+                if overlap < min_content_overlap:
+                    continue
+            else:
+                # No content words required (rare). Numbers alone OK.
+                pass
+
+            # 5. First qualifying window wins (don't over-search).
+            return (window_start, window_end)
+
+    return None
